docs.title: 🏗️ Engineering Principles
docs.text:
Quality is not a phase at the end of development, it's an engineering discipline embedded throughout the entire software delivery lifecycle.

Over the years, I've built my approach around principles that promote scalable, maintainable, and reliable software systems while enabling teams to deliver with confidence.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
• 📚 Documentation First – Create clear, accessible documentation that reduces knowledge silos and accelerates onboarding.
  • 🤖 Purposeful Automation – Automate repetitive, high-value tasks to improve efficiency, consistency, and release confidence.
  • 📊 Metrics-Driven Decisions – Use meaningful QA metrics and reporting dashboards to support informed decision-making.
  • 🔍 Risk-Based Testing – Prioritize testing efforts based on business impact, technical complexity, and potential risk.
  • 🤝 Cross-Functional Collaboration – Partner closely with Product, Development, Customer Support, and other stakeholders to build quality into every stage of delivery.
  • 🔄 Continuous Improvement – Continuously refine QA processes, automation frameworks, and documentation to improve team effectiveness.
  • 🛡️ Quality Beyond Functionality – Treat security, performance, reliability, accessibility, and usability as essential aspects of software quality.

Engineering Philosophy

Every automation framework, reporting dashboard, documentation system, and engineering solution showcased throughout this portfolio was built around these principles.

Quality is not something that is inspected into software at the end.

It is engineered into every stage of the software development lifecycle.

docs.title: My Engineering Approach
docs.text:
Every project I work on follows a structured quality mindset:

docs.title: Career Journey
docs.text: Every engineer has a story. Mine is one of curiosity, continuous learning, leadership, and a passion for delivering quality software.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
My career has been shaped by a simple goal: helping teams build better software.

From manual testing to QA leadership, automation engineering, documentation, reporting systems, and quality strategy, each stage of my journey has expanded both my technical expertise and my understanding of how quality contributes to successful software delivery.

Today, I approach Quality Engineering as a combination of technical excellence, collaboration, continuous improvement, and engineering discipline.

Career Timeline

🎓 Computer Engineering

→ 🔍 Manual Software Testing

→ 🤖 Test Automation

→ 👥 QA Team Leadership

→ 🏗️ Quality Engineering

→ 🚀 Continuous Learning & Engineering Excellence

docs.title: Where It All Began
docs.text:
My journey into technology started with a strong interest in solving problems and understanding how software works behind the scenes.

Studying Computer Engineering provided me with a solid technical foundation, but I quickly discovered that building software was only part of the equation. Delivering reliable, high-quality software required disciplined testing, structured processes, and a commitment to continuous improvement.

That realization sparked my interest in Software Quality Assurance and set me on a path toward becoming a Quality Engineer focused on building confidence into every stage of software delivery.

docs.title: Discovering Quality Engineering
docs.text:
I began my career focusing on manual software testing, learning the importance of understanding business requirements, identifying defects, and validating user expectations.

This stage helped me develop essential skills in:

  • Functional Testing
  • Regression Testing
  • User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
  • Test Case Design
  • Bug Reporting
  • Requirements Analysis
  • Software Documentation

Working closely with developers, business analysts, and project managers taught me that quality is a shared responsibility across the entire software development lifecycle. This experience taught me that successful testing is not just about finding defects, it's about understanding business needs and helping teams deliver software users can trust.

docs.title: Growing Beyond Manual Testing
docs.text:
As projects became larger and more complex, I recognized the need for automation to improve testing efficiency and reliability.

This led me to invest significant time in learning:

  • JavaScript
  • TypeScript
  • Cypress
  • Playwright
  • API Testing
  • SQL Validation

I started building reusable automation frameworks that reduced repetitive manual work and improved regression testing coverage.

Automation quickly became more than writing test scripts, it became an opportunity to improve engineering workflows, increase regression coverage, and enable faster, more reliable software releases.

docs.title: Taking on Leadership
docs.text:
As my experience grew, I transitioned into leadership roles where my responsibilities expanded beyond testing.

I began leading QA activities by:

  • Planning testing activities
  • Defining QA processes
  • Mentoring junior QA engineers
  • Reviewing bug reports
  • Coordinating regression testing
  • Improving documentation
  • Supporting release planning
  • Promoting quality best practices

Leading a QA team strengthened my appreciation for communication, mentorship, process improvement, and building a culture where quality is everyone's responsibility.

docs.title: Broadening My Engineering Expertise
docs.text:
To become a more effective Quality Engineer, I broadened my knowledge beyond functional testing.

I developed practical experience in:

  • REST API Testing
  • Performance Testing using Apache JMeter
  • SQL Database Validation
  • Security Testing using OWASP ZAP
  • CI/CD with GitHub Actions
  • Technical Documentation
  • Automation Framework Architecture

These skills enabled me to contribute throughout the software delivery lifecycle rather than focusing solely on testing.

docs.title: Building Better QA Processes
docs.text:
Beyond testing, I became increasingly interested in improving how teams work.

I designed and maintained engineering resources including:

  • Test Strategies
  • Test Plans
  • QA Documentation
  • Bug Reporting Standards
  • Test Templates
  • Checklists
  • Release Validation Processes
  • Knowledge Bases
  • QA Workflows

I also developed automated QA dashboards using Google Sheets to help teams track testing progress, defect trends, and quality metrics more efficiently.

docs.title: Continuous Learning
docs.text:
Continuous learning has been a defining part of my career.

I regularly invest in expanding my technical expertise through:

  • Professional Certifications
  • Hands-on Engineering Projects
  • Technical Documentation
  • Community Learning
  • Industry Best Practices

Current areas of focus include:

  • Advanced Test Automation
  • Performance Engineering

  • DevOps
  • AI-assisted Testing
  • Software Architecture

docs.title: Looking Ahead
docs.text:
As software engineering continues to evolve, I aim to keep growing as a Quality Engineering professional who combines technical expertise with leadership and continuous improvement.

My goal is to design scalable quality processes, build engineering solutions that reduce manual effort, mentor other QA professionals, and help organizations deliver reliable software with greater speed and confidence.

I believe the future of Quality Engineering lies in combining automation, engineering best practices, data-driven decision making, and collaborative problem solving, and I'm excited to continue contributing to that future.

docs.title: API Testing
docs.text: Ensuring reliable system communication through structured API validation, automation, and backend verification.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Modern applications rely heavily on APIs to exchange data between systems.

Testing these interfaces is essential to ensure business processes function correctly, data is exchanged accurately, and integrations remain stable throughout the software lifecycle.

My API testing approach combines manual validation, automation, and backend verification to provide confidence in application behaviour.

docs.title: Areas of Focus
docs.text:
My API testing activities include:

  • REST API Testing
  • Request & Response Validation
  • Status Code Verification
  • JSON Response Validation
  • Authentication Testing
  • CRUD Operations
  • API Regression Testing
  • API Automation
  • SQL Backend Validation

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
I use several tools depending on project requirements:

  • Postman
  • Swagger / OpenAPI
  • Cypress
  • Playwright
  • SQL

docs.title: Typical Workflow
docs.text:
A typical API testing process includes:

  1. Review API documentation.
  2. Understand request parameters.
  3. Validate authentication.
  4. Verify response structure.
  5. Confirm business rules.
  6. Validate backend data where applicable.
  7. Automate repeatable scenarios.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
Effective API testing helps teams:

  • Detect defects early
  • Validate business logic
  • Improve integration reliability
  • Reduce production issues
  • Support automation efforts

docs.title: Explore
docs.text:
• REST API Testing
  • Postman
  • Swagger / OpenAPI
  • API Automation
  • Authentication Testing

"Reliable APIs form the foundation of reliable software."

docs.title: Authentication Testing
docs.text: Verifying that only authorised users and systems can securely access application resources.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Authentication testing validates that users and systems can securely access protected resources using approved authentication mechanisms.

It helps ensure that access controls function correctly while preventing unauthorised access.

docs.title: Areas Covered
docs.text:
Typical authentication testing includes:

  • Login Validation
  • Logout Functionality
  • Session Management
  • Token Validation
  • Access Control
  • Role-based Access
  • Invalid Credential Handling

docs.title: Common Authentication Methods
docs.text:
Modern applications commonly use:

  • Username & Password
  • Bearer Tokens
  • API Keys
  • OAuth
  • JWT (JSON Web Tokens)

docs.title: Validation Activities
docs.text:
Typical verification includes:

  • Successful authentication
  • Invalid credentials
  • Missing authentication
  • Expired tokens
  • Invalid tokens
  • Permission validation
  • Session timeout
  • Logout verification

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
Authentication testing also verifies that:

  • Sensitive information is protected
  • Error messages do not expose unnecessary details
  • Sessions are properly invalidated after logout
  • Access is restricted to authorised users

"Strong authentication is the first line of defence for every application."

docs.title: API Automation
docs.text: Building repeatable automated API tests that improve software quality and support continuous delivery.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
API automation enables rapid validation of backend services by executing repeatable tests without manual intervention.

Automated API testing increases regression coverage while providing fast feedback during software development.

docs.title: Why Automate APIs?
docs.text:
API automation provides several advantages:

  • Faster execution
  • Reliable regression testing
  • Early defect detection
  • CI/CD integration
  • Reduced manual effort

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
Depending on project requirements, API automation may be implemented using:

  • Cypress
  • Playwright
  • Postman Collections
  • Newman

docs.title: Typical Validations
docs.text:
Automated API tests verify:

  • Status Codes
  • Response Body
  • Response Headers
  • Authentication
  • Response Time
  • Business Rules
  • Data Integrity

docs.title: Continuous Integration
docs.text:
API automation integrates well into CI/CD pipelines by providing rapid feedback after every code change.

This helps identify issues before software reaches later stages of testing.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
Automated API testing supports:

  • Stable releases
  • Faster regression testing
  • Increased confidence
  • Better collaboration
  • Continuous quality improvement

"Reliable APIs deserve reliable automated tests."

docs.text: Simplifying API development, testing, and collaboration.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Postman is one of the primary tools I use for manual API testing and exploratory validation.

It enables rapid verification of endpoints before they are incorporated into automated test suites.

docs.title: Typical Activities
docs.text:
Using Postman, I perform:

  • Endpoint Validation
  • CRUD Operations
  • Authentication Testing
  • Header Validation
  • Query Parameter Testing
  • Environment Configuration
  • Collection Management

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
Postman helps teams:

  • Debug issues
  • Document endpoints

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
Typical workflow:

  1. Import API documentation.
  2. Configure environments.
  3. Execute requests.
  4. Validate responses.
  5. Save reusable collections.
  6. Share with team members.

"Effective API testing begins with understanding the contract between systems."

docs.title: REST API Testing
docs.text: Validating API functionality, business rules, and data integrity across modern web applications.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
REST APIs enable communication between applications and services.

Testing these interfaces ensures that requests are processed correctly, responses meet expectations, and business rules are enforced consistently.

docs.title: Validation Areas
docs.text:
During REST API testing, I typically verify:

  • Request Parameters
  • Headers
  • Authentication
  • Status Codes
  • Response Body
  • Response Time
  • Error Handling
  • Business Logic

docs.title: Common HTTP Methods
docs.text:
Typical API requests include:

  • GET
  • POST
  • PUT
  • PATCH
  • DELETE

Each method is validated according to expected application behaviour.

docs.title: Response Validation
docs.text:
Testing includes verification of:

  • HTTP Status Codes
  • JSON Structure
  • Required Fields
  • Data Types
  • Response Messages
  • Business Rules

docs.title: Backend Verification
docs.text: Where appropriate, API responses are validated against backend databases to ensure data consistency and integrity.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Effective REST API testing includes:

  • Positive Scenarios
  • Negative Scenarios
  • Boundary Testing
  • Error Validation
  • Security Validation
  • Regression Testing

"APIs should be tested with the same discipline as user interfaces."

docs.title: Swagger / OpenAPI
docs.text: Using API documentation to improve testing accuracy and collaboration.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Well-documented APIs improve communication between developers, testers, and stakeholders.

Swagger (OpenAPI) provides a structured way to understand available endpoints, request formats, response models, and authentication requirements.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
Swagger helps testers:

  • Understand endpoints
  • Review request schemas
  • Validate response structures
  • Identify required parameters
  • Explore API capabilities

docs.title: Typical Usage
docs.text:
During testing, I use Swagger to:

  • Review endpoint documentation
  • Validate request payloads
  • Understand expected responses
  • Verify business rules
  • Plan automation scenarios

docs.title: Collaboration
docs.text:
Accurate API documentation benefits the entire engineering team by reducing ambiguity and improving consistency during development and testing.

"Good documentation makes good testing possible."

docs.title: Test Automation
docs.text: Building reliable, scalable, and maintainable automation frameworks that accelerate software delivery while improving product quality.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Test Automation is a key part of modern Quality Engineering.

As applications become more complex and release cycles become shorter, automation enables teams to validate software quickly, consistently, and confidently.

My approach focuses on building reusable automation frameworks rather than simply automating individual test cases.

docs.title: Why Test Automation?
docs.text:
Automation helps engineering teams:

  • Reduce repetitive manual testing
  • Increase regression coverage
  • Improve testing consistency
  • Detect defects earlier
  • Support Continuous Integration
  • Accelerate software delivery

Automation complements manual testing rather than replacing it.

docs.title: DevOps
docs.text: Supporting reliable software delivery through continuous integration, automation, collaboration, and release quality.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Modern software development relies on close collaboration between development, testing, and operations.

As a Quality Assurance Engineer, I contribute to DevOps practices by integrating testing into development workflows, supporting release validation, and promoting continuous quality throughout the software lifecycle.

docs.text:

  • Git & GitHub
  • Continuous Integration (CI)
  • GitHub Actions
  • Docker
  • Release Validation

docs.text:
Quality Assurance contributes to DevOps by:

  • Supporting Continuous Testing
  • Validating Release Builds
  • Automating Regression Testing
  • Providing Fast Feedback
  • Improving Deployment Confidence
  • Collaborating Across Teams

docs.title: Technologies
docs.text:

docs.title: Docker
docs.text: Creating consistent development and testing environments through containerisation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Docker simplifies application deployment by packaging software and its dependencies into portable containers.

From a QA perspective, containerisation improves consistency across development, testing, and production environments.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
Docker helps teams:

  • Standardise environments
  • Reduce configuration issues
  • Improve deployment consistency
  • Simplify testing

docs.title: QA Perspective
docs.text:
Containerised environments support:

  • Reliable Testing
  • Faster Environment Setup
  • Repeatable Test Execution
  • Better Collaboration

"Consistent environments reduce unexpected testing issues."

docs.title: Git & GitHub
docs.text: Managing source code, collaboration, and version history throughout the software development lifecycle.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Version control is an essential part of modern software engineering.

Git enables teams to collaborate efficiently while maintaining a complete history of project changes.

docs.title: Typical Activities
docs.text:
My workflow includes:

  • Cloning repositories
  • Creating feature branches
  • Committing changes
  • Pulling updates
  • Resolving merge conflicts
  • Reviewing pull requests
  • Managing releases

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
Git supports:

  • Collaboration
  • Traceability
  • Change Management
  • Code Reviews
  • Release Management

"Version control is the foundation of collaborative software development."

docs.title: GitHub Actions
docs.text: Automating software quality through Continuous Integration workflows.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
GitHub Actions enables engineering teams to automate testing and validation whenever code changes are introduced.

Integrating automated testing into CI pipelines helps detect issues early while improving release confidence.

docs.title: Typical CI Workflow
docs.text:
A typical pipeline may include:

  • Install Dependencies
  • Build Application
  • Execute Automated Tests
  • Generate Reports
  • Publish Results

docs.title: QA Benefits
docs.text:
Continuous Integration helps:

  • Detect regressions early
  • Reduce manual effort
  • Improve release confidence
  • Accelerate feedback
  • Support continuous delivery

docs.title: Automation Integration
docs.text:
Automated pipelines commonly execute:

  • Cypress Tests
  • Playwright Tests
  • API Tests
  • Smoke Tests

"Continuous Integration delivers continuous confidence."

docs.title: Release Validation
docs.text: Confirming software readiness before production deployment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Release validation is the final quality checkpoint before software reaches end users.

It provides confidence that new features, bug fixes, and system changes meet business and technical expectations.

docs.title: Typical Activities
docs.text:
My release validation process includes:

  • Smoke Testing
  • Regression Testing
  • Critical Workflow Verification
  • API Validation
  • SQL Verification

docs.title: Apache JMeter
docs.text: Simulating realistic workloads to evaluate application performance, reliability, and scalability.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Apache JMeter is an open-source performance testing tool used to simulate user activity and measure how applications behave under different workloads.

I use JMeter to assess system responsiveness, identify bottlenecks, and support performance optimisation before software is released.

docs.title: Performance Testing Types
docs.text: Using JMeter, I perform different types of performance testing depending on project objectives.

docs.title: Load Testing
docs.text:
Evaluates how an application performs under expected user traffic.

Typical objectives include:

  • Measuring response time
  • Validating throughput
  • Monitoring resource utilisation
  • Detecting bottlenecks

docs.title: Stress Testing
docs.text: Pushes an application beyond its expected operating capacity to determine its breaking point and evaluate recovery behaviour.

docs.title: Spike Testing
docs.text: Simulates sudden increases and decreases in user traffic to observe how the application responds to unexpected workload changes.

docs.title: Endurance Testing
docs.text: Runs the application under sustained load over an extended period to identify memory leaks, resource exhaustion, or performance degradation.

docs.title: Typical JMeter Components
docs.text:
My performance test plans commonly include:

  • Thread Groups
  • HTTP Request Samplers
  • CSV Data Set Config
  • Assertions
  • Timers
  • Listeners
  • Variables
  • Controllers

docs.title: Typical Workflow
docs.text:
A typical performance testing workflow includes:

  1. Define performance objectives.
  2. Design realistic user scenarios.
  3. Configure test data.
  4. Execute performance tests.
  5. Monitor application behaviour.
  6. Analyse reports.
  7. Share findings with stakeholders.

docs.title: Reporting
docs.text:
Typical reports include:

  • Average Response Time
  • Median Response Time
  • 90th / 95th Percentile
  • Throughput
  • Error Percentage
  • Active Users
  • Requests per Second

docs.title: Business Value
docs.text:
Performance testing helps teams:

  • Improve scalability
  • Detect bottlenecks early
  • Increase release confidence
  • Enhance user experience
  • Reduce production performance issues

"Reliable software should perform well not only under normal conditions, but also under pressure."

docs.title: Performance Metrics
docs.text: Measuring application performance using meaningful engineering metrics.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Performance metrics provide objective insight into how an application behaves under different workloads.

Rather than relying on assumptions, engineering teams can use measurable data to identify bottlenecks, compare releases, and make informed optimisation decisions.

docs.title: Response Time
docs.text:
The time taken for the application to respond to a request.

Lower response times generally improve user experience.

docs.title: Throughput
docs.text:
The number of requests or transactions processed within a given period.

Higher throughput usually indicates better system capacity.

docs.title: Error Rate
docs.text:
The percentage of failed requests during testing.

Monitoring error rates helps identify system instability under load.

docs.title: Concurrent Users
docs.text: The number of users actively interacting with the application at the same time.

docs.title: Transactions Per Second (TPS)
docs.text: Measures how many business transactions the application completes each second.

docs.title: CPU Utilisation
docs.text:
Tracks processor usage during testing.

High CPU utilisation may indicate performance bottlenecks.

docs.title: Memory Usage
docs.text:
Monitors memory consumption throughout test execution.

Unexpected increases may indicate memory leaks or inefficient resource management.

docs.title: Network Performance
docs.text: Evaluates data transfer efficiency between clients and servers.

docs.title: Why Performance Metrics Matter
docs.text:
Monitoring these metrics helps teams:

  • Identify bottlenecks
  • Compare application versions
  • Validate optimisation efforts
  • Support capacity planning
  • Improve scalability
  • Increase release confidence

docs.title: Continuous Improvement
docs.text:
Performance testing is most valuable when metrics are monitored consistently across releases, allowing engineering teams to identify trends and continuously improve system performance.

"Good engineering decisions are driven by measurable data, not assumptions."

docs.title: 🔌 API Testing & Validation
docs.text: Designing and executing reliable API testing strategies to ensure backend services are secure, performant, and function correctly before reaching end users.

docs.title: Project Overview
docs.text:
Modern applications rely heavily on APIs to power web, mobile, and third-party integrations. Throughout my QA career, I have designed and executed API testing strategies that validate backend functionality independently of the user interface.

By testing APIs early in the development lifecycle, I help identify defects before they impact frontend functionality, improving release confidence, reducing debugging effort, and supporting faster software delivery.

My approach combines functional validation, backend verification, security checks, and reusable automation to ensure APIs are reliable, secure, and production-ready.

Throughout my career, I've maintained structured defect management practices that improve collaboration between QA, developers, product owners, and stakeholders. My approach emphasizes clarity, reproducibility, and business impact, ensuring every defect report contributes to faster resolution and higher software quality.

docs.title: 🚀 CI/CD for Quality Engineering
docs.text: Integrating quality assurance into Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery pipelines to enable faster, safer, and more reliable software releases.

Modern Quality Assurance goes beyond writing automated tests. It involves integrating quality checks directly into the software delivery pipeline so defects are detected early and releases remain reliable.

I design automation frameworks with CI/CD in mind, enabling automated UI, API, and regression testing to run consistently on every code change. This approach provides rapid feedback to development teams, reduces release risk, and supports continuous delivery.

docs.text: The framework contains several reusable engineering solutions that solve real-world testing challenges.

docs.title: 📧 Automated Email OTP Login
docs.text:
Modern applications frequently require one-time passwords (OTP) during authentication.

To eliminate manual intervention, I designed an automated login flow that integrates:

  • Cypress
  • Auth0
  • Mailinator

The automated authentication workflow:

  1. Enters the test email
  2. Retrieves the OTP from Mailinator
  3. Extracts the verification code
  4. Completes authentication
  5. Stores the authenticated session
  6. Continues test execution without further user interaction

This approach enables reliable testing of secure authentication workflows while significantly reducing execution time.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: Read the full engineering article

docs.title: 🔑 Session Management with cy.session()
docs.text:
Repeated login operations slow down automated test suites and increase execution costs.

To improve efficiency, I implemented reusable authenticated sessions using cy.session().

Benefits include:

  • Login once
  • Reuse authenticated state
  • Faster regression testing
  • Reduced OTP requests
  • Improved test stability
  • Lower execution time

This significantly reduced overall suite execution time while improving reliability for regression and CI/CD pipelines.

docs.title: Implementation

docs.title: 🔌 API Testing with cy.api()
docs.text:
While Postman is well suited for exploratory API testing, I wanted automated API validation to live alongside UI automation within a single engineering workflow.

Using cypress-plugin-api, I integrated API testing directly into the Cypress framework.

This provides:

  • Visual request inspection
  • Response validation
  • Status code verification
  • Header inspection
  • Payload assertions
  • Integrated reporting

Running API tests alongside UI tests simplifies maintenance and keeps all automation within a single codebase.

docs.title: ⚡ Performance Engineering
docs.text: Designing and executing performance tests to evaluate application scalability, stability, reliability, and responsiveness under varying workloads.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Performance testing plays a critical role in ensuring applications remain responsive, stable, and scalable under both expected and peak workloads.

Using Apache JMeter, I have designed and executed performance tests to evaluate application behavior, identify bottlenecks, validate performance requirements, and provide actionable recommendations that improve software reliability before production deployment.

My approach combines structured test planning, realistic workload simulation, detailed analysis, and data-driven reporting to support informed engineering decisions.

docs.title: Performance Testing Objectives
docs.text:
• Measure application response times
  • Validate system stability under load
  • Identify performance bottlenecks
  • Evaluate scalability
  • Support release readiness
  • Improve user experience

docs.title: Technologies
docs.text: Tool Purpose Apache JMeter Load & Performance Testing CSV Data Sets Test Data HTTP Request Samplers API/Web Testing Listeners Result Analysis Assertions Validation HTML Reports Performance Reporting

docs.title: Engineering Approach
docs.text:
Every performance assessment follows a structured methodology:

  1. Understand performance requirements
  2. Define realistic workloads
  3. Build reusable JMeter test plans
  4. Execute baseline testing
  5. Increase load incrementally
  6. Monitor application behavior
  7. Analyze bottlenecks
  8. Provide optimization recommendations
  9. Re-test after improvements

docs.title: Baseline Testing
docs.text: Establish expected system performance under normal operating conditions before conducting more intensive testing.

docs.title: Load Testing
docs.text: Validate application performance under expected user load.

docs.title: Stress Testing
docs.text: Determine system breaking points under extreme traffic.

docs.title: Spike Testing
docs.text: Evaluate how the application reacts to sudden traffic increases.

docs.title: Endurance Testing
docs.text: Measure stability during prolonged execution.

docs.title: Scalability Testing
docs.text: Assess how the application scales as workload increases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
As I expanded my automation expertise beyond Cypress, I began exploring Playwright to better understand modern browser automation and cross-browser testing.

This project reflects that journey by applying clean architecture, reusable components, and modern software engineering practices to create automation that remains easy to extend as applications evolve.

docs.title: Applying the Page Object Model
docs.text:
One of the most valuable lessons while learning Playwright was understanding the importance of the Page Object Model (POM).

At first, creating separate page classes seemed unnecessary.

Why create another file when all the interactions could live inside a single test?

The answer became clear as projects grew.

Instead of scattering selectors across multiple test files, user interactions are encapsulated into reusable page methods that can be shared throughout the framework.

For example, rather than repeatedly writing:

await page.getByLabel('Username').fill(username);

await page.getByLabel('Password').fill(password);

await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' }).click();

the test simply becomes:

await loginPage.login(username, password);

This improves readability, reduces duplication, and makes UI changes much easier to maintain.

docs.text: Read the full engineering article

docs.title: Locator Strategies
docs.text:
Playwright provides several powerful locator strategies, allowing tests to closely mimic real user interactions.

Examples include:

  • getByRole()
  • getByLabel()
  • getByPlaceholder()
  • getByText()
  • CSS Selectors
  • XPath

Using semantic locators improves test reliability and resilience against UI changes.

docs.title: Cross-Browser Testing
docs.text:
One of Playwright's greatest strengths is its native support for multiple browser engines.

The framework supports testing across:

  • Chromium
  • Firefox
  • WebKit

This enables early detection of browser-specific issues without requiring additional tooling.

Running the same tests across Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit helps identify browser-specific issues early, improving confidence before release.

docs.title: Engineering Impact
docs.text:
This framework was designed to:

  • Improve test maintainability through reusable page objects
  • Reduce duplicated automation code
  • Simplify future UI updates
  • Enable reliable cross-browser validation
  • Support scalable test development as applications grow

docs.title: Reporting & Debugging
docs.text:
Playwright includes powerful debugging capabilities that simplify investigation when tests fail.

Features include:

  • HTML Reports
  • Trace Viewer
  • Screenshots
  • Video Recording
  • Step-by-step Execution
  • Network Inspection

These capabilities provide detailed visibility into every test execution, making failures easier to reproduce, diagnose, and resolve.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
Building this framework reinforced one important engineering principle:

Automation is not just about executing tests—it is about designing software.

Good automation frameworks apply the same engineering principles used in application development:

  • Separation of concerns
  • Reusability
  • Readability
  • Maintainability
  • Scalability

Understanding these concepts has helped me build automation that is easier to extend and maintain over time.

docs.title: 🛡️ Security Testing with OWASP ZAP
docs.text: Security is most effective when it becomes part of the engineering process, not an afterthought. My approach combines functional testing with vulnerability assessment to help identify security risks before production deployment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
In addition to functional and automation testing, I perform baseline security assessments using OWASP ZAP to identify common web application vulnerabilities.

My goal is to identify common security weaknesses early in the software delivery lifecycle, enabling engineering teams to remediate issues before production. While this does not replace a dedicated penetration test, it complements functional testing by integrating baseline application security checks into the QA process.

docs.title: 🧠 Knowledge Management System (KMS)
docs.text: A centralized engineering Knowledge Management System (KMS) built using Wiki.js to support onboarding, product documentation, QA processes, collaboration, and secure knowledge sharing across engineering teams.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Throughout my QA leadership journey, I designed, implemented, and administered internal Knowledge Management Systems (KMS) that became the single source of truth for engineering teams.

Rather than allowing critical knowledge to become scattered across emails, chat messages, spreadsheets, and individual documents, I centralized engineering documentation into a structured, searchable platform built with Wiki.js.

These systems supported Quality Assurance, Product Management, Business Analysis, Engineering, and Operations teams by making critical information easy to discover, maintain, and share.

The result was faster onboarding, improved collaboration, standardized processes, higher documentation quality, and long-term knowledge retention across the organization.

docs.title: Why We Built It
docs.text:
As engineering teams grow, valuable knowledge can easily become scattered across emails, chats, shared drives, and individual team members.

The Knowledge Management System solved this problem by providing one central location where employees could find accurate, up-to-date documentation whenever they needed it.

Instead of repeatedly asking the same questions, team members could quickly search the knowledge base and find standardized documentation.

This approach promoted self-service learning, reduced dependency on tribal knowledge, improved process consistency, and significantly accelerated employee onboarding.

docs.title: Challenges Solved
docs.text:
The Knowledge Management System addressed several common engineering challenges:

  • Documentation scattered across multiple locations
  • Inconsistent onboarding experiences
  • Knowledge loss when team members changed roles
  • Repeated support questions
  • Outdated documentation
  • Difficulty locating engineering information

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Quality Assurance is more than identifying defects,it's about building confidence in software before it reaches users.

Throughout my career, I have contributed to projects across government agencies, registry platforms, and private-sector organisations, ensuring applications meet functional, business, and quality expectations.

My approach combines structured testing, automation, documentation, collaboration, and continuous improvement.

docs.title: Core QA Activities
docs.text:
My experience covers the complete software testing lifecycle, including:

  • Requirement Analysis
  • Test Planning
  • Test Strategy
  • Functional Testing
  • Regression Testing
  • Smoke Testing
  • User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
  • SQL Backend Validation
  • API Testing
  • Defect Management
  • Release Validation

docs.title: Quality Philosophy
docs.text:
Quality should be considered from the beginning of software development,not only during testing.

By collaborating with developers, business analysts, and stakeholders throughout the development lifecycle, potential issues can be identified earlier, reducing risk and improving delivery confidence.

docs.title: Areas Covered
docs.text:
This section explores the practices and artefacts that support effective software quality.

  • Test Strategy
  • Test Plans
  • QA Templates
  • QA Checklists
  • Bug Management
  • QA Dashboards

docs.title: Guiding Principles
docs.text:
Every QA initiative I contribute to is guided by a few core principles:

  • Understand the business requirements.
  • Prioritise risk.
  • Test what matters most.
  • Communicate clearly.
  • Document consistently.
  • Continuously improve processes.

docs.title: Explore
docs.text:
• Test Strategy
  • Test Plans
  • Templates
  • Checklists
  • Bug Management
  • QA Dashboards

"Quality is achieved through discipline, collaboration, and continuous improvement, not by chance.

docs.title: Bug Management
docs.text: Identifying, documenting, prioritising, tracking, and verifying software defects throughout the development lifecycle.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Effective defect management is essential for delivering reliable software.

A good bug report does more than describe an issue—it provides developers with the information they need to reproduce, understand, and resolve the problem efficiently.

docs.title: My Bug Reporting Process
docs.text:
Every defect I report includes:

  • Clear Title
  • Environment
  • Module
  • Build Version
  • Severity
  • Priority
  • Preconditions
  • Steps to Reproduce
  • Expected Result
  • Actual Result
  • Supporting Evidence
  • Additional Notes

docs.title: Severity vs Priority
docs.text: I distinguish between:

docs.title: Severity
docs.text:
The technical impact of the defect.

Examples:

  • Critical
  • High
  • Medium
  • Low

docs.title: Priority
docs.text:
How urgently the defect should be resolved.

Examples:

  • Immediate
  • High
  • Medium
  • Low

docs.title: Defect Lifecycle
docs.text:
A typical workflow includes:

  1. New
  2. Assigned
  3. In Progress
  4. Fixed
  5. Ready for Testing
  6. Verified
  7. Closed

If necessary:

  • Reopened

docs.title: Good Bug Reports
docs.text:
Effective bug reports should be:

  • Reproducible
  • Concise
  • Accurate
  • Evidence-based
  • Easy to understand

Supporting evidence may include:

  • Screenshots
  • Screen recordings
  • Logs
  • API responses
  • SQL queries

docs.title: Collaboration
docs.text:
Bug management is a collaborative activity involving:

  • QA Engineers
  • Developers
  • Business Analysts
  • Product Owners
  • Project Managers

Clear communication helps teams resolve issues more efficiently.

"A well-written bug report saves hours of investigation."

docs.title: QA Dashboards
docs.text: Transforming testing data into meaningful insights that support informed decision-making.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
One of my favourite engineering projects has been designing automated Quality Assurance dashboards using Google Sheets.

These dashboards replace manual reporting with live metrics, enabling stakeholders to monitor testing progress in real time.

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
The dashboards were designed to:

  • Reduce manual reporting
  • Improve visibility
  • Track testing progress
  • Support release decisions
  • Improve team communication

docs.title: Metrics Tracked
docs.text:
Examples include:

  • Test Execution Progress
  • Passed Tests
  • Failed Tests
  • Blocked Tests
  • Not Executed Tests
  • Defect Status
  • Defect Trends
  • Test Coverage
  • Release Readiness

docs.title: Dashboard Features
docs.text:
The dashboards include:

  • Interactive Charts
  • Conditional Formatting
  • Progress Indicators
  • Summary Tables
  • Live Calculations
  • Automated Metrics

docs.title: Business Value
docs.text:
The dashboards provide several benefits:

  • Faster reporting
  • Better visibility
  • Improved planning
  • Reduced manual effort
  • Increased stakeholder confidence

docs.title: Future Improvements
docs.text:
As reporting needs evolve, dashboards can be expanded to include:

  • Sprint Metrics
  • Automation Coverage
  • Test Execution History
  • Release Trends
  • Quality KPIs
  • Performance Metrics

"Good metrics don't just measure quality,they help improve it."

docs.title: Test Plans
docs.text: A structured roadmap for validating software functionality, reducing project risk, and supporting successful software releases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A Test Plan describes how software will be tested during a project.

It defines the testing scope, objectives, approach, responsibilities, environments, schedules, and expected deliverables.

A well-prepared Test Plan improves communication across teams while ensuring that testing activities remain organised and aligned with project goals.

docs.title: Typical Test Plan Contents
docs.text:
My Test Plans typically include:

  • Project Overview
  • Objectives
  • Scope
  • Test Items
  • Features to be Tested
  • Features Not to be Tested
  • Test Approach
  • Test Environment
  • Test Data
  • Roles & Responsibilities
  • Entry Criteria
  • Exit Criteria
  • Risks & Mitigation
  • Deliverables
  • Approval

docs.title: Test Levels
docs.text:
Depending on the project, testing activities may include:

  • Smoke Testing
  • Functional Testing
  • Regression Testing
  • Integration Testing
  • User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
  • API Testing
  • SQL Validation

docs.title: Risk Management
docs.text:
Every Test Plan includes an assessment of project risks.

Examples include:

  • Business-critical functionality
  • Schedule constraints
  • Environment instability
  • Incomplete requirements
  • Third-party dependencies

Understanding these risks helps prioritise testing effort effectively.

docs.title: Deliverables
docs.text:
Typical outputs include:

  • Approved Test Plan
  • Test Cases
  • Defect Reports
  • Test Execution Reports
  • Test Summary Report
  • Release Recommendation

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
A structured Test Plan helps teams:

  • Improve communication
  • Reduce project risk
  • Increase testing consistency
  • Support successful releases
  • Build stakeholder confidence

"A successful release begins with a well-structured test plan."

docs.title: Test Strategy
docs.text: A structured approach to ensuring software quality through effective planning, risk assessment, and collaboration.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
A Test Strategy defines the overall approach to software testing for a project.

It ensures testing activities align with business objectives while providing confidence that the product meets quality expectations.

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
A typical test strategy aims to:

  • Validate business requirements
  • Reduce project risk
  • Improve software quality
  • Detect defects early
  • Support successful releases

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Testing activities may include:

  • Functional Testing
  • Regression Testing
  • Smoke Testing
  • Integration Testing
  • User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
  • API Testing
  • SQL Validation

docs.title: Risk-Based Testing
docs.text:
Not all features carry the same level of business risk.

Testing effort should focus on:

  • Critical business workflows
  • High-usage functionality
  • Financial transactions
  • Authentication and authorisation
  • Data integrity

This approach helps maximise quality while using testing time efficiently.

docs.title: Entry Criteria
docs.text:
Testing begins when:

  • Requirements are approved
  • Test environment is available
  • Test data is prepared
  • Builds are stable

docs.title: Exit Criteria
docs.text:
Testing concludes when:

  • Planned test cases are executed
  • Critical defects are resolved
  • Regression testing is complete
  • Stakeholders approve release readiness

docs.title: Deliverables
docs.text:
Typical outputs include:

  • Test Strategy
  • Test Plan
  • Test Cases
  • Defect Reports
  • Test Summary Report
  • Release Recommendation

"An effective test strategy aligns quality objectives with business goals."

docs.title: Security Testing
docs.text: Improving software quality by validating security controls, identifying common vulnerabilities, and supporting secure software delivery.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Security testing helps ensure that software protects sensitive information, enforces proper access controls, and resists common security threats.

While security specialists perform deep penetration testing, Quality Assurance teams play an important role in verifying that applications implement security requirements correctly throughout development.

docs.title: Areas Covered
docs.text:
My security testing activities include:

  • Authentication Testing
  • Authorization Verification
  • Input Validation
  • Session Management
  • Secure Configuration Checks
  • OWASP Top 10 Awareness
  • OWASP ZAP Scanning
  • API Security Validation

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
Security testing aims to:

  • Reduce application risk
  • Protect user data
  • Validate access controls
  • Detect common vulnerabilities
  • Support secure software releases

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
Security-related testing may involve:

  • OWASP ZAP
  • Browser Developer Tools
  • Postman
  • Swagger / OpenAPI

docs.title: Explore
docs.text:
• OWASP ZAP
  • OWASP Top 10

"Building secure software begins with verifying security throughout development."

docs.title: OWASP Top 10
docs.text: Understanding the most significant security risks affecting modern web applications.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The OWASP Top 10 is a widely recognised awareness document that highlights the most critical web application security risks.

Understanding these risks helps QA engineers design more effective test scenarios and identify potential weaknesses during testing.

docs.title: Common Categories
docs.text:
The current OWASP Top 10 includes risks such as:

  • Broken Access Control
  • Cryptographic Failures
  • Injection
  • Insecure Design
  • Security Misconfiguration
  • Vulnerable Components
  • Identification & Authentication Failures
  • Software & Data Integrity Failures
  • Security Logging & Monitoring Failures
  • Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

docs.title: QA Perspective
docs.text:
Quality Assurance contributes by verifying:

  • Authentication behaviour
  • Authorisation rules
  • Input validation
  • Error handling
  • Secure session management
  • API security controls

docs.title: Continuous Improvement
docs.text:
Security awareness is an ongoing process.

Integrating security checks into regular QA activities helps reduce risk and supports the delivery of more resilient software.

"Security is a shared responsibility across the entire engineering team."

docs.title: OWASP ZAP
docs.text: Using automated security scanning to identify common web application vulnerabilities.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy) is an open-source security testing tool that helps identify common web application vulnerabilities.

As part of a QA workflow, it can provide valuable insights into potential security issues before deployment.

docs.title: Typical Activities
docs.text:
Using OWASP ZAP, I perform:

  • Passive Scanning
  • Active Scanning (where appropriate)
  • Security Header Validation
  • Authentication Checks
  • Session Analysis
  • Vulnerability Review

docs.title: Common Findings
docs.text:
Typical issues identified include:

  • Missing Security Headers
  • Cookie Configuration Issues
  • Information Disclosure
  • Insecure Authentication Flows
  • Input Validation Concerns

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
Using OWASP ZAP helps teams:

  • Detect security weaknesses early
  • Improve application security posture
  • Support secure release processes
  • Raise awareness of security best practices

"Security testing complements functional testing by helping identify risks before production."

docs.title: SQL Validation
docs.text: Verifying data integrity, business rules, and backend consistency through structured database validation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
User interfaces display information, but databases store the truth.

SQL validation plays an important role in ensuring that application data is stored, updated, retrieved, and processed correctly throughout the software lifecycle.

I use SQL to verify backend data, investigate defects, validate business rules, and confirm that application behaviour matches database records.

docs.title: Areas Covered
docs.text:
This section explores:

  • Backend Data Validation
  • Data Integrity
  • CRUD Verification
  • Stored Procedures
  • Query Validation

docs.title: Why SQL Matters
docs.text:
Database validation helps teams:

  • Verify application behaviour
  • Confirm data accuracy
  • Detect backend defects
  • Validate business rules
  • Investigate production issues

docs.title: Typical Activities
docs.text:
My SQL validation activities include:

  • Verifying inserted records
  • Confirming updated data
  • Validating deleted records
  • Checking relationships between tables
  • Comparing frontend and backend data
  • Investigating reported defects

docs.title: Explore
docs.text:
• Backend Validation
  • Data Integrity
  • Stored Procedures

"Reliable applications depend on reliable data."

docs.title: Backend Validation
docs.text: Confirming that application actions are accurately reflected within the database.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Backend validation verifies that data created or modified through the user interface is correctly stored in the underlying database.

This helps ensure consistency between the application and its data layer.

docs.title: Typical Validation Activities
docs.text:
Backend validation may include:

  • Record Creation
  • Record Updates
  • Record Deletion
  • Field Validation
  • Relationship Verification
  • Business Rule Validation

docs.title: Common Scenarios
docs.text:
Typical examples include:

  • Verifying successful registration
  • Confirming profile updates
  • Checking transaction records
  • Validating workflow changes
  • Ensuring audit information is stored correctly

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
Backend validation helps:

  • Detect hidden defects
  • Improve data accuracy
  • Support functional testing
  • Increase release confidence

"Testing should confirm not only what users see, but also what the system stores."

docs.title: Data Integrity
docs.text: Ensuring application data remains complete, accurate, and consistent.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Data integrity ensures that information remains reliable throughout its lifecycle.

Quality Assurance plays an important role in verifying that applications preserve data accuracy during normal operations.

docs.title: Areas Verified
docs.text:
Typical validation includes:

  • Required Fields
  • Data Relationships
  • Duplicate Prevention
  • Referential Integrity
  • Business Rules
  • Transaction Accuracy

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
Strong data integrity supports:

  • Reliable reporting
  • Accurate business processes
  • Better user confidence
  • Reduced production issues

"Quality software depends on trustworthy data."

docs.title: Stored Procedures
docs.text: Validating database procedures that support business processes and application functionality.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Many enterprise applications rely on stored procedures to process business logic within the database.

Quality Assurance includes verifying that these procedures produce accurate and consistent results.

docs.title: Typical Validation
docs.text:
Testing may include:

  • Input Validation
  • Output Verification
  • Business Rule Validation
  • Error Handling
  • Data Accuracy
  • Performance Observation

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
Stored procedure validation helps ensure:

  • Consistent business processing
  • Reliable database operations
  • Reduced data-related defects

"Database logic deserves the same level of validation as application code."
